title_en: Introduction to the Forty Hadith and the Commentary Tradition
title_ar: مقدمة في the Forty Hadith and the Commentary Tradition
content_en:
Imam Yahya ibn Sharaf al-Nawawi's (d. 676 AH / 1277 CE) collection of forty-two hadiths — known as al-Arba'een al-Nawawiyyah — is among the most memorized and studied texts in all of Islamic scholarship. Nawawi's stated intention was to compile hadiths that serve as 'pivots of Islam' (qawa'id kulliyyah) — foundational principles from which large portions of Islamic teaching radiate. Each hadith in the collection is a comprehensive statement that scholars have used to derive guidance across multiple domains of worship, ethics, law, and personal development.

The commentary tradition on the Forty Hadith is rich and spans centuries. The two most celebrated classical commentaries are by Ibn Daqiq al-Eid (d. 702 AH / 1302 CE) — whose brief but penetrating commentary focuses on linguistic analysis and legal derivation — and Ibn Rajab al-Hanbali (d. 795 AH / 1393 CE) — whose Jami' al-Ulum wal-Hikam is an expansive masterwork that traces the meanings of each hadith through the full breadth of Islamic scholarship. Contemporary commentaries by Ibn Uthaymin, Salih al-Fawzan, and others have made the collection accessible to modern students. This commentary chapter draws primarily from Ibn Daqiq al-Eid and Ibn Rajab.

The Forty Hadith begins with Nawawi's preface, in which he explains his methodology and cites the hadith of the Prophet, peace be upon him: 'Whoever preserves forty hadiths for my community from its religious affairs, Allah will resurrect him on the Day of Judgment among the scholars and jurisprudents.' Though the isnad of this hadith is disputed, scholars accepted it and the tradition of compiling forty-hadith collections became a flourishing genre in Islamic scholarship.

The collection opens with the most foundational hadith in all of Islam — the Hadith of Jibreel — which encapsulates the religion in three levels: Islam, Iman, and Ihsan. This hadith alone, scholars have said, is sufficient to convey the totality of the religion, making it the ideal opening for a collection designed to convey the essentials of Islamic teaching in condensed form.
title_id: Pengantar Empat Puluh Hadis dan Tradisi Komentar
content_id:
Koleksi empat puluh dua hadis karya Imam Yahya ibn Syaraf al-Nawawi (wafat 676 H / 1277 M) — yang dikenal sebagai al-Arba'een al-Nawawiyyah — termasuk salah satu teks yang paling banyak dihafal dan dipelajari dalam seluruh keilmuan Islam. Niat al-Nawawi yang dinyatakan adalah untuk mengompilasi hadis-hadis yang berfungsi sebagai 'poros-poros Islam' (qawa'id kulliyyah) — prinsip-prinsip dasar dari mana bagian-bagian besar ajaran Islam memancar. Setiap hadis dalam koleksi ini adalah pernyataan komprehensif yang digunakan oleh para ulama untuk menghasilkan panduan di berbagai domain ibadah, etika, hukum, dan pengembangan pribadi.

Tradisi komentar atas Empat Puluh Hadis ini sangat kaya dan mencakup berabad-abad. Dua komentar klasik yang paling terkenal adalah karya Ibnu Daqiq al-Eid (wafat 702 H / 1302 M) — yang komentarnya singkat namun tajam berfokus pada analisis linguistik dan derivasi hukum — dan Ibnu Rajab al-Hanbali (wafat 795 H / 1393 M) — yang Jami' al-'Ulum wal-Hikam-nya adalah mahakarya yang luas yang menelusuri makna setiap hadis melalui seluruh keluasan keilmuan Islam. Komentar-komentar kontemporer oleh Ibnu 'Utsaimin, Shalih al-Fauzan, dan lainnya telah membuat koleksi ini dapat diakses oleh pelajar modern. Bab komentar ini mengacu terutama pada Ibnu Daqiq al-Eid dan Ibnu Rajab.

Empat Puluh Hadis dimulai dengan kata pengantar al-Nawawi, di mana beliau menjelaskan metodologinya dan mengutip hadis Nabi ﷺ: 'Barangsiapa menjaga empat puluh hadis untuk umatku dari urusan agamanya, Allah akan membangkitkannya pada Hari Kiamat di antara para ulama dan ahli fiqih.' Meskipun sanad hadis ini diperdebatkan, para ulama menerimanya dan tradisi mengompilasi koleksi empat puluh hadis menjadi genre yang berkembang dalam keilmuan Islam.

Koleksi ini dibuka dengan hadis paling mendasar dalam seluruh Islam — Hadis Jibril — yang merangkum agama dalam tiga tingkatan: Islam, Iman, dan Ihsan. Hadis ini saja, kata para ulama, sudah cukup untuk menyampaikan totalitas agama, menjadikannya pembuka yang ideal bagi sebuah koleksi yang dirancang untuk menyampaikan esensi ajaran Islam dalam bentuk yang ringkas.
content_ar:
Imam Yahya ibn Sharaf al-Nawawi's (d. 676 AH / 1277 CE) collection of forty-two hadiths — known as al-Arba'een al-Nawawiyyah — is among the most memorized and studied texts in all of Islamic scholarship. Nawawi's stated intention was to compile hadiths that serve as 'pivots of Islam' (qawa'id kulliyyah) — foundational principles from which large portions of Islamic teaching radiate. Each hadith in the collection is a comprehensive statement that scholars have used to derive guidance across multiple domains of worship, ethics, law, and personal development.

The commentary tradition on the Forty Hadith is rich and spans centuries. The two most celebrated classical commentaries are by Ibn Daqiq al-Eid (d. 702 AH / 1302 CE) — whose brief but penetrating commentary focuses on linguistic analysis and legal derivation — and Ibn Rajab al-Hanbali (d. 795 AH / 1393 CE) — whose Jami' al-Ulum wal-Hikam is an expansive masterwork that traces the meanings of each hadith through the full breadth of Islamic scholarship. Contemporary commentaries by Ibn Uthaymin, Salih al-Fawzan, and others have made the collection accessible to modern students. This commentary chapter draws primarily from Ibn Daqiq al-Eid and Ibn Rajab.

The Forty Hadith begins with Nawawi's preface, in which he explains his methodology and cites the hadith of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم, peace be upon him: 'Whoever preserves forty hadiths for my community from its religious affairs, الله will resurrect him on the Day of Judgment among the scholars and jurisprudents.' Though the isnad of this hadith is disputed, scholars accepted it and the tradition of compiling forty-hadith collections became a flourishing genre in Islamic scholarship.

The collection opens with the most foundational hadith in all of Islam — the Hadith of Jibreel — which encapsulates the religion in three levels: Islam, Iman, and Ihsan. This hadith alone, scholars have said, is sufficient to convey the totality of the religion, making it the ideal opening for a collection designed to convey the essentials of Islamic teaching in condensed form.
